perturbation.original_text:
15. TERMINATION OF EMPLOYMENT
15.1 The Appointment may be terminated by either party giving the other at least 12 months' notice in writing.
perturbation.changed_text:
15. TERMINATION OF EMPLOYMENT
15.1 The Employment Contract may be terminated by either party giving the other at least 12 months' notice in writing.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'Appointment' in section 15.1 is being replaced with 'Employment Contract'. This introduces ambiguity as 'Appointment' is defined in section 1.1 but 'Employment Contract' is not defined, leading to potential confusion about which agreement this clause refers to. This contradicts the defined 'Appointment' term.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.1 In this Agreement: Appointment means the employment of the Executive by the Company on and subject to the terms of this Agreement; Board means the board of directors of the Company or any committee of the board duly appointed for the purpose in question, from time to time; Financial Year means the Company's financial year ending on 31 December each year; Group means the Company, any holding company of the Company, and any holding company of the holding company from time to time, together with any subsidiary of the Company or its holding company or the holding company of its holding company, and Group Company means any one of them; holding company and subsidiary shall, as the context so permits, have the meaning given by section 1159 of the Companies Act 2006 or under relevant applicable laws in Jersey; Recognised Investment Exchange means a relevant EEA market as defined in, or a market established under, the rules of any investment exchange specified in schedule 3 to the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Financial Promotion) Order 2005; Compensation Committee means the committee of non-executive directors as appointed by the Board of WPP plc from time to time for the purposes of determining the Company's policy on executive remuneration; Termination Date means the date on which the Appointment terminates for whatever reason; and UK Listing Authority means the FCA, acting in its capacity as the competent authority for the purposes of part VI of the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Appointment' to 'Employment Contract' in section 15.1 introduces ambiguity and contradicts section 1.1, which defines 'Appointment'. The original text aligns with the defined term in 1.1, while the changed text uses an undefined term that might or might not be equivalent to 'Appointment'. Since 'Employment Contract' is undefined, it's unclear if the terms are interchangeable, creating a contradiction because the original 'Appointment' is a specific, defined term, thus breaking the document's internal consistency and clarity of contractual obligations.

perturbation.original_text:
6. REMUNERATION
6.1 The Company will pay the Executive a salary of £740,000 and a fixed benefits allowance of £30,000 per annum. The salary (and so far, as is reasonably possible) the benefits allowance will accrue from day to day and be payable in equal instalments in arrears on or around the 25th day of every month, less deductions for income tax and National Insurance contributions and shall be inclusive of any fees receivable by the Executive as a director of any Group Company.
perturbation.changed_text:
6. REMUNERATION
6.1 The Company will provide the Executive a compensation of £740,000 and a fixed benefits allowance of £30,000 per annum. The compensation (and so far, as is reasonably possible) the benefits allowance will accrue from day to day and be payable in equal instalments in arrears on or around the 25th day of every month, less deductions for income tax and National Insurance contributions and shall be inclusive of any fees receivable by the Executive as a director of any Group Company.
perturbation.explanation: In section 6.1, 'salary' is replaced with 'compensation'. While both relate to payment, 'salary' is a specific term with established legal meaning. 'Compensation' is broader and could be interpreted to include other forms of payment not necessarily covered by the defined salary structure. This creates an in-text contradiction regarding what constitutes the Executive's remuneration, contradicting sub-clauses that specifically mention salary.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.2 The Executive's salary will be reviewed by the Compensation Committee every two years. There will be no salary review after notice to terminate this Agreement has been given by either party. The Company has no obligation to increase the Executive's salary following a review.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies 'salary', a defined term relating to fixed compensation. The changed text broadens this to 'compensation', a less specific term that could encompass various forms of payment. Section 6.2 refers specifically to 'salary reviews', implying a fixed and predictable remuneration. Replacing 'salary' with 'compensation' introduces ambiguity as to whether other forms of compensation, besides the fixed salary, are subject to review. This undermines the specificity of the review process outlined in 6.2, creating an in-text contradiction. The change from salary to compensation introduces ambiguity and potential conflict with the salary review process defined in section 6.2.

perturbation.explanation: In section 13.1, the key term 'Confidential Information' is replaced with 'Proprietary Data'. The rest of the section continues to list what constitutes 'Proprietary Data' *as if* it were 'Confidential Information', using the definition and examples clearly intended for 'Confidential Information'. Because these terms are not synonymous and because the text explicitly defines Confidential Information elsewhere (but does *not* define Proprietary Data'), the definition of Confidential Information no longer aligns with what it ought to mean, creating internal inconsistency and making it impossible to know how 'Confidential Information' is to be treated or interpreted. This contradiction affects all subsequent mentions of 'Confidential Information'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 13.2 The Executive must not make use of or divulge to any person or entity, and must use his best endeavours to prevent the unauthorised use, publication or disclosure of, any Confidential Information which is disclosed or made available to the Executive, either directly or indirectly, during the course of, or in connection with, the Executive's employment or his holding any office within the Group from any source within the Company or any Group Company and shall be under an obligation promptly to report to the Group any such unauthorised use or disclosure which comes to his knowledge.
perturbation.justification: The change replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Proprietary Data' in section 13.1, while the rest of the section continues to define what should have been 'Confidential Information' as if it were 'Proprietary Data', even though the text explicitly defines 'Confidential Information' elsewhere. This creates a direct conflict with section 13.2, which refers to 'Confidential Information' and imposes obligations related to it. Because 'Confidential Information' has been redefined as 'Proprietary Data' in 13.1, but this new term has not been defined in 13.2, it is unclear what obligations pertain to the originally defined term. This constitutes a Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction, as the document now uses the term 'Confidential Information' without a clear and consistent meaning, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction.
